overall_summary: Oracle, as a large data center operator, urges FERC to streamline interconnection and permitting procedures to accelerate AI infrastructure deployment in the United States. The filing calls for decision shot clocks, shorter comment periods, harmonized load shedding and downtime standards, and streamlined backstop permitting to reduce the regulatory burden data centers face when seeking power access.

quotes.text: One of the most significant and widespread delays Oracle encounters when siting data centers in the United States is access to sufficient energy.
quotes.concern: Energy access delays are the primary obstacle Oracle faces when siting U.S. data centers.

quotes.text: The Commission should support efforts to streamline administrative procedures and harmonize standards related to load sheds and down time.
quotes.concern: Oracle asks FERC to standardize load shedding and downtime rules across regions.

quotes.text: the Commission could explore imposing decision shot clocks on its own cases.
quotes.concern: Oracle requests binding timelines for FERC decisions to speed interconnection.

quotes.text: Shortening longer comment periods and limiting intervenor and reply comment rounds to a single round will allow for public participation without creating a heckler's veto.
quotes.concern: Oracle wants shorter comment periods and a single intervenor reply round to prevent procedural delay.

quotes.text: the Commission could explore ways to streamline backstop permitting procedures.
quotes.concern: Oracle supports faster backstop permitting to reduce siting delays.

quotes.text: the Commission could encourage ways to harmonize load shedding practices as well as downtime calculations.
quotes.concern: Oracle calls for uniform load shedding and downtime calculation rules to reduce regional patchwork.

quotes.text: Winning the AI race with an American tech stack requires powering data centers here in the United States and abroad as fast as possible.
quotes.concern: Oracle frames rapid data center power access as a national AI competitiveness imperative.

bins.name: Faster interconnection study process
bins.description: Oracle supports procedural reforms to speed FERC's own decision-making, including shot clocks and shorter comment periods. The filing links energy access delays directly to slower AI infrastructure deployment.

bins.name: Harmonized load shedding rules
bins.description: Oracle asks FERC to harmonize load shedding practices and downtime calculations across regions, arguing that the current patchwork of standards creates unnecessary friction for data center operators.

bins.name: Energy access as key barrier
bins.description: Oracle identifies insufficient energy access as the single most significant and widespread obstacle it faces when siting U.S. data centers, framing the rulemaking as an opportunity to close that gap.

bins.name: Uniform national standards
bins.description: Oracle argues that a patchwork of regional load shedding and downtime rules slows data center deployment and asks FERC to encourage harmonized national standards.

bins.name: Shot clocks and shorter proceedings
bins.description: Oracle requests that FERC impose decision shot clocks, shorten comment periods, limit intervenor rounds to one, and streamline backstop permitting to cut the time from application to decision.

bins.name: AI race and U.S. dominance
bins.description: Oracle frames the entire filing around U.S. AI competitiveness, arguing that powering data centers fast is essential to winning the AI race against global rivals.
